key_insight: Modularity only clearly helps continual learning when the model operates in a lower-dimensional, richer representational regime; in a high-dimensional lazy regime, modular and single-network models behave similarly.
engineering_relevance: For engineered learning systems, the result suggests that robustness and transfer depend less on hard module separation and more on how representational subspaces are allocated and shared across related tasks.
method: Compared a task-partitioned recurrent network against a single-network baseline.
Studied a sequential A-B-A continual learning setup to test stability vs. plasticity.
Induced high- and low-dimensional regimes by manipulating weight initialization scale.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that both architectures perform similarly in the high-dimensional lazy regime, while in the lower-dimensional rich regime the modular network forms task-specific subspaces that better reflect task similarity and improve compositional organization.
watch_item: The conclusions depend on initialization-scale-driven regime changes and a specific sequential A-B-A setup, so transfer to other architectures, task sequences, or real-world workloads may require revalidation.
paper_title: Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Helps in Continual Learning
